title: Not Everything You See Is True

required.title: [Read Aloud] Fake News and Ridiculous Rumours
required.author: or similar age-appropriate media literacy book

required.title: [For Mentor] Weaponized Lies: How to Think Critically in the Post-Truth Era
required.author: Daniel Levitin

suggested.title: [Read Aloud] The Boy Who Cried Wolf
suggested.author: any version

supplementary.title: [For Mentor] Common Sense Media resources on media literacy for young children

narration_prompts.prompt: Can you explain why not everything you see online is true?
narration_prompts.detail: Give me an example of how something could look true but not be.

narration_prompts.prompt: When you watch a video on a platform and the next one starts automatically, who picked that video?
narration_prompts.detail: Why did they pick it? What is the platform trying to do?

narration_prompts.prompt: A video has five million views. Does that mean it is true?
narration_prompts.detail: Why or why not? What does the number of views actually tell you?

narration_prompts.prompt: Can you think of a way a picture or video could be changed to be misleading?
narration_prompts.detail: How would you check if something you saw was the whole picture?
What does it pay attention to? What is it trying to do? Is it trying to show you what is true?

cross_connections.description: The concept that "someone decided to show you this" was introduced in 4.2.1-A and is fully developed here as the foundation of information literacy.

cross_connections.target_name: Screens Are Tools, Not Default Activities
cross_connections.description: Understanding that algorithms keep you watching connects directly to the attention discipline developed in 4.2.6-A. The child who knows the algorithm is trying to hold their attention is better equipped to choose when to stop.
"Popular does not mean true" is a concrete, age-appropriate version of the appeal-to-popularity fallacy introduced in 1.7.

The principle that "the story we are told is never the complete story" from history applies directly to digital information. Feeds are curated just as histories are written — by someone with choices and priorities.

content.why_this_matters:
Every day, you see things on screens — videos, pictures, stories, and posts. Some of these things are true. Some are not. Some are partly true but changed to make you feel a certain way. And here is the part most people never think about: you did not choose most of what you see. Someone — or something — decided to show it to you.

When you watch a video and the next one starts playing automatically, who picked that video? You did not. The platform picked it, because it thinks that video will keep you watching. When you see pictures in a feed, they are not in the order they were posted. The platform rearranged them to show you the ones it thinks you will react to.

This packet teaches you three things that will change how you look at every screen for the rest of your life: not everything you see online is true, someone chose to show it to you for a reason, and popular does not mean correct. These three ideas are simple, but most adults have never truly learned them.
content.what_this_develops:
1. **Awareness that online content can be false, misleading, or incomplete** — not everything that looks real is real
2. **The habit of asking "who decided to show me this?"** — understanding that platforms select and arrange content
3. **Understanding that popularity does not equal truth** — views, likes, and shares tell you about spread, not accuracy
4. **Basic recognition of edited or out-of-context content** — photos can be altered, videos can be cut to change meaning
5. **The beginning of a questioning habit** that becomes the foundation of information literacy
content.core_content:
### Part 1: Not Everything Online Is True

This seems obvious when you say it out loud. Of course not everything online is true. But in practice, most people believe what they see — especially when it comes with a picture, a video, or a lot of likes.

Here is why things online can be untrue:

**People can say anything.** Anyone can write anything and put it online. There is no test you have to pass before you post. There is no rule that says everything on the internet has to be true. Some people share things that are wrong because they believe them. Some people share things that are wrong on purpose, to trick others.

**Pictures can lie.** A photo can be changed using computer tools. People can be added or removed. Colors can be changed. Things can be moved. You might see a photo of something that looks real but never happened. Even photos that have not been changed can be misleading — a picture taken from one angle can look completely different from a picture of the same thing taken from another angle.

**Videos can be cut.** Imagine someone says: "I love ice cream, but I do not love broccoli." Now imagine a video that only shows the part where they say "I do not love broccoli." The video is real — those words were really said — but it leaves out important context. This happens constantly with videos online. A long speech gets cut to a few seconds. The few seconds that get shared might completely change what the person meant.

**The habit to build:** When you see something online, ask yourself: "Is this definitely true? How do I know? Could it have been changed?" You do not need to doubt everything — but you should check things that seem surprising, shocking, or too good to be true.

### Part 2: Someone Chose to Show You This

Remember what you learned in 4.2.1-A: what you see on a screen was put there by someone who made choices about what to show you. Now let us go deeper into what that means.

**The platform picks what you see.** When you use YouTube, TikTok, or any platform with a feed, the platform uses a system called an **algorithm** to decide what to show you. The algorithm watches what you click, what you watch, how long you watch it, and what makes you react. Then it shows you more of the same kind of thing.

The algorithm is not trying to show you what is true. It is not trying to show you what is important. It is trying to show you what will keep you watching. Things that keep people watching are often exciting, surprising, funny, scary, or outrage-inducing. They are not necessarily true, important, or good for you.

**Think about it this way:** If you watched a video about sharks and then watched another one, the algorithm learns: "This person likes shark videos." So it shows you more shark videos. Soon your feed is full of sharks, and you might start thinking sharks are everywhere and very dangerous. But that is not reality — it is your feed, shaped by what you clicked.

**The habit to build:** When you see something in your feed, ask: "Why am I seeing this? Did I go looking for it, or did the platform put it here?" This simple question breaks the illusion that your feed is just "what is happening in the world."

### Part 3: Popular Does Not Mean True

A video has ten million views. A post has a hundred thousand likes. Does that mean it is true?

No. It means it spread. Spreading and being true are completely different things.

Things spread because they make people feel strong emotions — surprise, anger, joy, fear. A story that makes you feel outraged gets shared more than a story that is accurate but boring. A video that shocks you gets more views than a video that calmly explains something. The things that spread fastest online are often the things designed to provoke the strongest reactions — and strong reactions are not the same as careful thinking.

**Real examples to think about:**
- A funny video with five million views might be completely staged — actors performing a scene, not real people in a real situation
- A "fact" post shared by thousands of people might be completely made up — the number of shares does not make it true
- A video with only fifty views might contain genuinely important, accurate information — the low view count says nothing about its quality

**The habit to build:** When you see something popular, do not assume it is true because many people saw it. Ask: "Is this true because it has lots of views, or does it have lots of views because it made people feel something?" These are very different things.

### Part 4: Real Versus Edited — Is This the Whole Picture?

You are going to encounter things online that look completely real but have been changed. Here are the most common ways:

**Photos can be altered.** Computer tools can change colors, remove objects, add things that were not there, or combine parts of different photos into one. Some of these changes are obvious. Some are invisible. You cannot always tell by looking.

**Videos can be cut to change meaning.** Taking a short clip from a long video can completely reverse the meaning. Someone being sarcastic can look serious. Someone joking can look angry. A question can be removed, making the answer seem like a random statement.

**Words can be taken out of context.** A sentence that makes perfect sense in a paragraph can mean something completely different on its own. This happens constantly online — someone pulls one sentence out of a long article, and that one sentence, separated from everything around it, creates a completely different impression.

**The questions to ask:**
- Is this the whole thing, or part of something bigger?
- Has this been changed or edited?
- What is the original context — where did this come from originally?

You will not always be able to answer these questions. That is fine. The point is to ask them — to develop the habit of questioning rather than automatically believing what appears on your screen.

mentor_guide.competence_evaluation:
### 1. Content Can Be False
**Developing well:** The child can articulate that not everything online is true and can give examples of how content can be false or misleading.

**Developing slowly:** The child agrees in principle but defaults to believing what appears on screen in practice.

**Diagnostic questions:** Show a surprising claim and ask: "Do you think this is true? How could we check?"

### 2. Someone Chose to Show You This
**Developing well:** The child understands that platforms select and arrange content using algorithms. They can ask "why am I seeing this?" as a genuine question.

**Developing slowly:** The child treats their feed as a natural, neutral representation of reality.

**Diagnostic questions:** "Who picked the next video that played? Why did they pick that one?"

### 3. Popular Does Not Mean True
**Developing well:** The child can explain that views and likes indicate spread, not accuracy. They do not treat high view counts as evidence of truth.

**Developing slowly:** The child equates popularity with validity — "it has a million views so it must be true."

**Diagnostic questions:** "If a video with no views says one thing and a video with ten million views says the opposite, which one is true?" (Answer: you cannot tell from the view counts.)
